peer_exemplars.reasoning: While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

High openness to experience and very high libertarian anti-censorship orientation drive support for transparency, but very high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control tempers it to moderate support.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) combined with very high agreeableness and trust (80.8) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low openness (10.8) and high system justification (80.2) temper enthusiasm for disruptive regulation.

High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm and high trust in institutions combine with high libertarian privacy concerns to produce clear support, though high authoritarian law-and-order preference prevents maximal endorsement.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.
Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.

Strong fairness/equality values (3.18 on equality facet, but high HEXACO fairness at 93.03) and high dutifulness (91.68) drive support for disclosure to protect information integrity, though high collective narcissism (87.69) slightly reduces concern about foreign bots.

Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Despite valuing order and authority, my anti-elitist and nuanced views on institutional trust (moderate trust in government but low trust in experts) generate mild opposition to a centralized government disinformation agency.

Despite high trust in government, very high libertarian skepticism of state control and high anti-domination moral foundation create mild opposition to a centralized agency.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.
Very low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission are offset by low trust in government and a dangerous worldview, producing mild opposition to a new agency.
Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.

Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.

Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is nearly neutralized by low openness and privacy skepticism, resulting in a near-ambivalent score.
Low openness and high privacy skepticism (libertarianism) create mild opposition to granting broad researcher access, despite some authoritarian support for oversight.

Very high libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high openness to experience push toward preferring freedom from moderation, but very high authoritarian law-and-order preference and high trust in institutions pull back to near-neutral.

Very high libertarianism (89.8) and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) combined with high liberty/oppression moral foundation (98.03) strongly favor minimal moderation, though high agreeableness (89.41) softens the preference.

Very high authoritarian submission (92%), conventionalism (81%), and punitive attitudes (17.5% but in context of high RWA) combine with low openness and high conscientiousness to produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring strong platform rules to maintain social order.
